title: System Design Interview Concepts for Senior Software Engineer
subtitle: An interactive mind map of core topics, sub-topics, and trade-offs.

children.title: Scalability and Load
children.note: How to grow a system to handle more traffic and data.

children.title:
**Definition:** Scale-up approach that increases capacity by adding more resources (CPU, RAM, storage, IOPS) to a single machine.
**Trade-offs:** Simple with no application changes and avoids distributed-system complexity, but is bounded by hardware ceilings, becomes exponentially expensive, and leaves a Single Point of Failure (SPOF).
**Application Example:** Upgrading a primary OLTP database server to a larger AWS RDS instance class (e.g., db.r6g.large to db.r6g.16xlarge) to absorb traffic spikes before sharding.

**Definition:** Scale-out approach that adds more machine instances behind a load balancer instead of growing one machine.
**Trade-offs:** Removes the SPOF and scales nearly linearly, but requires the application to be stateless and adds operational complexity (deployments, partial failures, more network hops).
**Application Example:** An auto-scaling group of 20 stateless API pods behind an AWS ALB, with HPA adding pods when CPU > 70%.
Load Balancers
children.note: Orchestrator that distributes traffic across instances.

children.title:
**Definition:** Sends each new request to the next backend in a fixed rotation.
**Trade-offs:** Trivial and uniform when servers are equal, but ignores actual server load — a slow backend gets the same share as a fast one.
**Application Example:** An nginx `upstream` block fronting four equally-sized stateless workers with the default round-robin policy.

**Definition:** Routes each new request to the backend currently holding the fewest active connections.
**Trade-offs:** Naturally balances long-lived sessions and slow clients, but requires the LB to track per-backend state, which is harder in distributed LB setups.
**Application Example:** HAProxy with `balance leastconn` in front of WebSocket servers where some clients hold connections for hours.

**Definition:** Hashes the client IP and consistently maps it to the same backend.
**Trade-offs:** Cheap stickiness without a session store, but breaks when clients share a NAT/IP and rebalances poorly when the backend pool changes.
**Application Example:** A legacy PHP app needing in-process session affinity, fronted by an nginx upstream configured with `ip_hash`.

**Definition:** Load balancing at the transport layer — routes by IP and port, blind to HTTP semantics.
**Trade-offs:** Extremely fast and protocol-agnostic, but cannot route on URL/headers/cookies or terminate TLS at the LB.
**Application Example:** AWS NLB fronting a Postgres or Redis cluster where smart routing is unnecessary and ultra-low latency matters.

**Definition:** Load balancing at the application layer — inspects HTTP requests to route by host, path, header, or cookie.
**Trade-offs:** Enables smart routing (path-based, A/B, canary) and TLS termination, but is slower than L4 and limited to HTTP-like protocols.
**Application Example:** AWS ALB / nginx routing `/api/*` to one service and `/static/*` to another, with a 5% weighted canary to a new release.

**Definition:** Architectural property where each app instance keeps no client-specific state in memory; any instance can serve any request.
**Trade-offs:** Enables horizontal scaling and zero-downtime deploys, but pushes session, cart, and personalization state to an external store (Redis, JWT, DB), adding a dependency.
**Application Example:** A stateless Node.js cluster on Kubernetes with sessions in Redis — pods can be killed and replaced without users noticing.
CAP and PACELC Theorems
children.note: Trade-offs under network partitions and normal operation.

children.title:
**Definition:** Every successful read returns the most recent committed write, or an error.
**Trade-offs:** Eliminates stale-read confusion, but requires synchronous replication or quorums, increasing latency and reducing availability under partitions.
**Application Example:** A bank ledger replicated synchronously so a balance read from any region always reflects the last debit, even if it slows reads to ~50ms.

**Definition:** Every request receives a non-error response, even if some replicas are unreachable.
**Trade-offs:** Maximizes uptime, but allows reads of stale data during partitions.
**Application Example:** A DynamoDB-backed shopping cart that always lets the user add items, even if the write hasn't replicated to all regions yet.

**Definition:** The system continues operating despite arbitrary message loss or delays between nodes.
**Trade-offs:** Mandatory for any real distributed system (networks always partition), but forces a CAP choice between Consistency and Availability during the partition.
**Application Example:** A multi-region database where the EU and US regions each keep accepting traffic if the WAN link drops, then reconcile when it returns.

**Definition:** Extends CAP — under Partition you choose A or C; **E**lse (no partition) you still choose between **L**atency and **C**onsistency.
**Trade-offs:** Forces an honest discussion of normal-operation trade-offs that CAP alone glosses over.
**Application Example:** Choosing Cassandra (PA/EL) for a recommendation feed where 5ms p99 matters more than reading the absolute latest write.
Databases and Storage
children.note: Pick the model that fits the access pattern.

children.title: SQL (Relational, ACID)
children.note: Structured data, complex joins, strong consistency. ACID guarantees make these the safe default for money, inventory, and anything with invariants you cannot violate.

children.title:
**Definition:** A transaction is all-or-nothing — either every operation persists or none of them do.
**Trade-offs:** Eliminates partial-update bugs but requires undo logs and rollback machinery, costing throughput and storage.
**Application Example:** A money-transfer transaction that debits A and credits B in one BEGIN/COMMIT block; a crash mid-transfer leaves both balances unchanged.

**Definition:** Every committed transaction leaves the database in a state satisfying all declared constraints (PKs, FKs, checks, triggers).
**Trade-offs:** Catches data-integrity bugs at write time, but enforcing constraints — especially across rows or tables — takes locks and CPU.
**Application Example:** A FOREIGN KEY from `orders.customer_id` to `customers.id` rejects an order whose customer was already deleted, preventing orphan rows.

**Definition:** Concurrent transactions are isolated so the result is equivalent to running them in some serial order.
**Trade-offs:** Tunable from Read Uncommitted to Serializable — stronger isolation prevents anomalies (dirty/non-repeatable/phantom reads) but reduces concurrency.
**Application Example:** PostgreSQL `SERIALIZABLE` for a seat-booking system to prevent two users from booking the same seat under concurrent transactions.

**Definition:** Once a transaction commits, its effects survive crashes, power loss, and restarts.
**Trade-offs:** Guarantees no committed data is ever lost, but every commit pays the cost of an `fsync` to disk and replication.
**Application Example:** PostgreSQL's WAL is `fsync`-flushed before COMMIT returns and shipped to a synchronous standby for additional durability.

**Definition:** Mature relational systems implementing SQL + ACID — PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle.
**Trade-offs:** Decades of tooling, mature replication, and rich query optimizers — but vertical-scale-first and harder to shard than NoSQL siblings.
**Application Example:** PostgreSQL as the primary OLTP store for a fintech app, using strict isolation for ledgers and read replicas for reporting.

**Definition:** Schema-less store mapping keys to opaque values, optimized for O(1) lookup.
**Trade-offs:** Extremely fast and simple, but offers no rich query language and no joins — every access pattern must reduce to a key.
**Application Example:** Redis cache holding `session:{id} → user_json`, and DynamoDB storing `user_id → profile` for a mobile backend.

**Definition:** Stores nested JSON-like documents indexed by ID, with secondary indexes and ad-hoc queries on document fields.
**Trade-offs:** Flexible schemas accelerate iteration and match nested aggregates well, but lack of joins or cross-document transactions can push complexity into the app.
**Application Example:** MongoDB collection of e-commerce products where each document holds variants, images, and pricing rules together.

**Definition:** Stores data column-by-column rather than row-by-row, optimized for scans/aggregates over a few columns of many rows.
**Trade-offs:** Massive compression and analytical query speed-ups, but write/update-heavy workloads and single-row lookups are slow.
**Application Example:** BigQuery or Snowflake powering BI dashboards over a few-billion-row events table; Cassandra for time-series telemetry.

**Definition:** Models data as nodes and edges, optimized for traversal queries (shortest path, friend-of-friend, recommendations).
**Trade-offs:** Vastly faster than SQL self-joins for deep traversals, but smaller ecosystem, harder ops, and overkill if relationships are shallow.
**Application Example:** Neo4j powering a fraud-detection feature that asks "is this account within 3 hops of a known bad actor?".

**Definition:** Read-only DB copies kept in sync with the primary that absorb SELECT traffic.
**Trade-offs:** Cheap horizontal scaling for reads with no app changes, but introduces replication lag — reads can be stale and writes still bottleneck on the primary.
**Application Example:** A reporting service pointed at PostgreSQL replicas; writes go to the primary while heavy analytical SELECTs land on a replica.

**Definition:** B-tree, hash, GIN, and similar indexes plus query rewriting that avoid full table scans.
**Trade-offs:** Reads get orders-of-magnitude faster, but every index slows writes and consumes storage; bad indexes can hurt more than help.
**Application Example:** Adding a composite index on `(user_id, created_at)` to turn a 4-second order-history query into a 5-millisecond one.
Caching Strategies
children.note: Trade freshness for latency at multiple layers.

children.title:
**Definition:** App always reads from the cache; on a miss the cache itself fetches from the DB, populates, and returns.
**Trade-offs:** App code is simple and consistent, but the first read after a cold start is slow, and the cache library must understand how to query the source.
**Application Example:** AWS ElastiCache (Redis) used in read-through mode in front of DynamoDB for product-catalog reads.

**Definition:** Every write goes synchronously to both the cache and the DB before returning success.
**Trade-offs:** Cache always reflects the latest data, but writes pay two round-trips and writes that never get read waste cache space.
**Application Example:** A user-profile service writing to PostgreSQL and a Redis user-cache so the next profile read is always a cache hit.

**Definition:** Writes hit the cache only; the cache asynchronously flushes batched changes to the underlying DB.
**Trade-offs:** Extremely fast writes that absorb spikes, but a cache crash before flush risks **data loss** — needs durable cache, replication, or an outbox.
**Application Example:** A high-volume metrics ingest pipeline buffering counters in Redis and flushing aggregates to ClickHouse every 5 seconds.

**Definition:** App checks the cache; on a miss, queries the DB and explicitly writes the result back.
**Trade-offs:** Most flexible and the de-facto Redis pattern, but the app must invalidate stale entries and handle the thundering herd of misses on hot keys.
**Application Example:** A blog rendering service: `redis.get(post_id)` → on miss `db.query(...)` then `redis.setex(post_id, 300, value)`.
Eviction Policies
children.note: When the cache is full, which key gets dropped?

children.title:
**Definition:** Eviction policy that drops the entry untouched for the longest time when the cache is full.
**Trade-offs:** Excellent default — exploits temporal locality — but pathological under one-time scans that evict the genuinely-hot working set.
**Application Example:** Redis configured with `maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru` for a session/object cache where recent users are likely active again.

**Definition:** Eviction policy that drops the entry with the lowest hit count, regardless of recency.
**Trade-offs:** Better than LRU when popularity is stable, but old hits keep new entries out and shifting popularity is hard to adapt to without decayed counters.
**Application Example:** Redis `allkeys-lfu` for a CDN edge cache where a small set of evergreen assets dominates traffic.

**Definition:** Each entry carries an expiry timestamp; the entry is evicted once that time passes regardless of usage.
**Trade-offs:** Bounds staleness without any access tracking, but doesn't react to memory pressure — a cache with all-fresh entries can still fill up.
**Application Example:** Auth-token cache with a 15-minute TTL — tokens age out automatically without explicit invalidation logic.

**Definition:** FIFO evicts the oldest insert; Random picks any entry uniformly.
**Trade-offs:** Both are O(1) and trivial, but ignore access patterns and usually underperform LRU/LFU on real workloads.
**Application Example:** Embedded systems or fallbacks (e.g., Redis `maxmemory-policy allkeys-random`) where simplicity outweighs efficiency.
Layers
children.note: Caches stack — closer to the user = faster + cheaper.

children.title:
**Definition:** Geographically-distributed PoPs caching static assets close to end users at the network edge.
**Trade-offs:** Dramatic latency wins (10–50ms vs. 200ms+) and origin offload, but invalidation is harder farther from origin and dynamic content is harder to cache.
**Application Example:** Cloudflare/Fastly fronting an SPA — JS/CSS/images served from the nearest PoP with `Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable` for hashed bundles.

**Definition:** Cache lives inside the service process or a shared store like Redis/Memcached, accessed in microseconds.
**Trade-offs:** Sub-ms reads for hot DB queries and computed views, but consistency between instances is your problem and memory is finite.
**Application Example:** A pricing service holding a Redis cache of `sku → price`, populated lazily and invalidated by a Kafka topic of price-change events.

**Definition:** The DB engine caches plans, page buffers, and result sets in its own RAM.
**Trade-offs:** Mostly automatic — the DBA tunes RAM and the app gets free wins — but invalidated aggressively on writes and not a substitute for an app-level cache.
**Application Example:** PostgreSQL's `shared_buffers` sized to ~25% of RAM so the working set lives in memory and disk reads are rare on hot tables.
Messaging and Event-Driven Design
children.note: Decouple producers from consumers; absorb spikes; build resilience.

children.title: Architectural comparison — with vs. without Msg/EDD
children.note: The same use case ("a user signs up") looks very different in each style.

children.title: WITHOUT Messaging / EDD (synchronous chain)
children.note: **API → Auth → Email → Billing → Analytics**, all in one request. Pros: trivial to reason about, easy to test, immediate feedback. Cons: tight coupling — if Billing is down the signup fails; latency = sum of all services; spikes overwhelm downstream; hard to add a 6th step without touching the API.

children.title:
**Definition:** Failure mode of a synchronous chain — cascading errors and timeouts that propagate back to the user.
**Trade-offs:** Easy to reason about during incidents (one stack trace), but every downstream blip becomes a user-facing failure.
**Application Example:** A signup endpoint returning 500 because the analytics service is down, even though signup itself technically succeeded.

**Definition:** Scenarios where synchronous chains are the right call — small systems, few services, low traffic, strong-consistency demand.
**Trade-offs:** Simpler architecture and cheaper to operate, but quickly outgrown as services and traffic grow.
**Application Example:** A 3-service internal admin tool where every request is HTTP and the team optimizes for ramp-up speed, not throughput.
WITH Messaging / EDD (asynchronous events)
children.note: **API → Auth (commits) → publishes `UserSignedUp`**. Email, Billing, Analytics each subscribe and react independently. Pros: producers don't know consumers; queues absorb spikes; one consumer can fail/retry without breaking signup; trivial to add a 6th subscriber. Cons: eventual consistency, harder debugging (distributed traces become essential), need idempotent consumers and a dead-letter strategy.

children.title:
**Definition:** Failure mode with messaging — growing queue lag and duplicate deliveries, but the core path stays up.
**Trade-offs:** Resilient to downstream outages, but you trade clear stack traces for distributed-trace investigations and idempotency requirements.
**Application Example:** After a Stripe outage, the `payments` consumer's lag spikes to 4 hours, then drains; users still completed signup and the payment confirms when Stripe recovers.

**Definition:** Scenarios where event-driven design pays off — many independent consumers, spiky load, audit/event-sourcing needs.
**Trade-offs:** Loose coupling and elasticity at the cost of eventual consistency and harder debugging.
**Application Example:** An e-commerce platform where `OrderPlaced` triggers shipping, billing, fraud-check, and analytics — each scaling and failing independently.

**Definition:** Point-to-point messaging where a message is delivered to exactly one consumer from a worker pool.
**Trade-offs:** Reliable work distribution and back-pressure absorption, but harder to add new consumers without coordinated changes.
**Application Example:** SQS holding image-resize jobs picked up by an autoscaling pool of workers.

**Definition:** Topic-based messaging where one published message fans out to many independent subscribers.
**Trade-offs:** Producers and consumers are fully decoupled and new consumers can be added at any time, but each subscriber must independently track failures and offsets.
**Application Example:** A `UserSignedUp` topic on Kafka feeding the email, billing, and analytics services in parallel.

**Definition:** Distributed, partitioned, replicated commit log that retains messages and allows replay by offset.
**Trade-offs:** Massive throughput and replayability for event-sourcing, but heavier ops (Zookeeper/KRaft, brokers) and a learning curve compared to classic queues.
**Application Example:** A clickstream platform ingesting 1M events/sec into a `pageviews` topic, with downstream stream-processors and S3 archivers reading at their own pace.

**Definition:** Classic AMQP broker with rich exchange/binding routing semantics and per-message acks.
**Trade-offs:** Flexible routing (direct, topic, fanout, headers) and operationally simple, but worse at high-volume retention/replay than Kafka.
**Application Example:** A SaaS app routing webhook deliveries through topic exchanges so per-tenant retry policies can be applied.

**Definition:** Pushing slow or unreliable work onto a queue so the request thread returns instantly while a worker handles it later.
**Trade-offs:** Fast user response and resilience to worker outages, but introduces eventual consistency and the need for status polling/notifications.
**Application Example:** A "Generate PDF report" button that enqueues a job and emails the user when ready, instead of blocking the HTTP request for 30 seconds.
Sharding and Partitioning
children.note: Split a large dataset across nodes to remove the single-DB bottleneck.

children.title:
**Definition:** Partitioning that assigns a contiguous range of the key space to each shard (e.g., users `A–F`, `G–M`).
**Trade-offs:** Range queries are efficient and routing is simple, but skewed data or sequential keys create hot ranges.
**Application Example:** A time-series DB sharded by week — the current week's shard takes nearly all writes while older shards are cold.

**Definition:** Hashes the key and modulos by the number of shards to assign each key to a shard.
**Trade-offs:** Even data distribution, but range queries become scatter-gather and adding/removing shards reshuffles most of the data.
**Application Example:** A user-profile DB sharded by `hash(user_id) % 64` so writes spread evenly across 64 shards.

**Definition:** A lookup directory or geographic key (region, tenant ID) decides which shard owns each row.
**Trade-offs:** Locality wins (EU data on EU shards) and explicit control, but the directory becomes a hot dependency that needs its own HA story.
**Application Example:** A SaaS app where each customer's data lives entirely on one shard chosen by `tenant_id` from a `tenants → shard_id` directory.

**Definition:** Maps both keys and shards onto a circular hash space so adding/removing a shard only affects neighbors.
**Trade-offs:** Minimizes data movement during rebalances, at the cost of slightly less even distribution unless virtual nodes are used.
**Application Example:** DynamoDB and Cassandra use consistent hashing internally so scaling out to a new node moves only ~1/N of the data.

**Definition:** One shard receiving disproportionately more traffic or data than the others.
**Trade-offs:** The bottleneck even with many shards; mitigations (key salting, write sharding) trade simplicity for balance.
**Application Example:** A celebrity profile that dwarfs every other user — solved by salting the key (`user_id#1`, `user_id#2`, …) and reading the shards in parallel.
Consistency trade-offs
children.note: Once your data is split across nodes, **strong consistency becomes expensive**. The hard parts are not lookups — they're the operations that span shards.

children.title:
**Definition:** Operations whose data lives entirely on one shard inherit that shard's local ACID guarantees.
**Trade-offs:** Fast and consistent, but only attainable when the schema and access patterns are designed around the shard key.
**Application Example:** All of one user's posts on the same shard — `INSERT post` and `UPDATE counts` run as a single local transaction.

**Definition:** Atomic writes spanning multiple shards require **2-Phase Commit (2PC)** or distributed-coordination protocols.
**Trade-offs:** Provide strong consistency across shards but slow, block on coordinator failure, and reduce availability.
**Application Example:** A money transfer where sender and receiver live on different shards — requires 2PC or a saga; most teams avoid 2PC entirely.

**Definition:** Replace one distributed transaction with a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action on failure.
**Trade-offs:** Buys availability and decoupling at the cost of eventual consistency and the operational complexity of compensations.
**Application Example:** Booking a trip = book flight → book hotel → book car; if the car fails, run "cancel hotel" and "cancel flight" compensations.

**Definition:** Reads that need data from multiple shards executed as scatter-gather queries.
**Trade-offs:** Latency is bounded by the slowest shard; results may reflect different points in time, leading to read-your-writes anomalies.
**Application Example:** An "all my orders across regions" query that fans out to every regional shard and merges results in the API gateway.

**Definition:** Moving data while traffic flows so shards stay balanced as the cluster grows or shrinks.
**Trade-offs:** Necessary as the system evolves, but during the move reads can hit either old or new locations — design for double-writes, idempotency, and a controlled cutover.
**Application Example:** Vitess re-sharding a 4-shard MySQL cluster to 8 shards using filtered logical replication and per-key cutover.
Choosing the shard key matters most

**Definition:** The single design decision that determines hot spots, query patterns, and rebalance pain.
**Trade-offs:** A good key matches the dominant access pattern; a bad one creates hot shards or forces every query to fan out.
**Application Example:** Choosing `(tenant_id, created_at)` instead of just `created_at` so each tenant's data co-locates and per-tenant queries hit one shard.

**Definition:** Heuristic for sharded systems — keep transactional boundaries inside one shard, accept eventual consistency across shards.
**Trade-offs:** Trades a "perfect" relational model for a system that scales — clients only see idempotent cross-shard operations.
**Application Example:** An e-commerce app where each order, items, and inventory updates live on the same shard, and cross-warehouse reports are eventually consistent.
Microservices and Communication
children.note: Independent services collaborating over the network.

children.title:
**Definition:** HTTP-based, resource-oriented API style using verbs (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) and JSON.
**Trade-offs:** Universally supported, cacheable, and easy to debug, but verbose, weakly typed, and chatty for fine-grained data.
**Application Example:** A public-facing `/api/v1/orders/{id}` endpoint consumed by web, mobile, and third-party integrations.

**Definition:** Schema-first, binary RPC over HTTP/2 using Protocol Buffers.
**Trade-offs:** Fast, strongly typed, supports streaming, but harder to debug from a browser and not natively cacheable.
**Application Example:** Internal service-to-service calls in a microservices fleet where 40% lower latency and codegen across 5 languages outweigh debuggability concerns.

**Definition:** Query language that lets clients request exactly the fields they need from a single endpoint.
**Trade-offs:** Eliminates over-fetching and works for diverse clients, but server-side query analysis (depth limits, cost) and caching are more complex than REST.
**Application Example:** A mobile app fetching only the 4 fields it needs from a 50-field user object via a GraphQL `/graphql` endpoint.
Service Discovery
children.note: **The problem:** in a dynamic system, service instances come and go (auto-scaling, deploys, crashes). Hard-coding `billing-service:8080` doesn't work — that IP/port changes constantly. Service Discovery is how a caller finds **a healthy instance** of a target service at runtime.

children.title:
**Definition:** Live database of `service-name → [healthy instances]` updated by self-registration and health checks.
**Trade-offs:** Foundational for dynamic systems, but is itself a critical dependency — must be HA and quickly converge after partitions.
**Application Example:** A Consul cluster registering 200 service instances and exposing their addresses via DNS or HTTP API.

**Definition:** The act of asking the registry for an instance of a target service at call time.
**Trade-offs:** Avoids hard-coded addresses and supports dynamic scaling, but adds a registry lookup on every call (usually cached).
**Application Example:** Service A's HTTP client resolving `billing.default.svc.cluster.local` via cluster DNS before each request, with a 30-second cache.

**Definition:** The caller queries the registry, picks an instance, and applies its own load-balancing logic.
**Trade-offs:** One fewer hop and smart client routing, but every language/runtime needs the discovery library and routing logic is duplicated.
**Application Example:** Netflix Eureka + Ribbon — Spring Cloud apps fetch the live instance list and round-robin among them in the client process.

**Definition:** The caller hits a fixed address (LB / gateway / sidecar) that internally consults the registry.
**Trade-offs:** Clients stay dumb and language-agnostic, but the LB itself must be HA and adds a hop.
**Application Example:** A Kubernetes Service IP that kube-proxy uses to round-robin across the live pods behind it — the client sees one stable address.

**Definition:** Built-in K8s mechanism that gives every Service a stable DNS name backed by kube-proxy load balancing.
**Trade-offs:** Service discovery for free if you're already on K8s, but coupling with the cluster — cross-cluster needs extra mesh/federation.
**Application Example:** A `payments` Service exposed as `payments.default.svc.cluster.local`; pods come and go but the DNS name stays.

**Definition:** HashiCorp's standalone registry with health checks, KV store, multi-datacenter, and DNS/HTTP lookup.
**Trade-offs:** Stronger feature set than K8s DNS (cross-DC, KV, ACLs), but a separate component to run and secure.
**Application Example:** A multi-cloud setup where workloads in AWS and on-prem register in a Consul federation and discover each other across both.

**Definition:** Netflix's client-side discovery registry, popular in older Spring Cloud stacks.
**Trade-offs:** Battle-tested in Netflix-scale systems, but largely superseded by K8s-native DNS and service meshes.
**Application Example:** A legacy Spring Cloud microservices app using Eureka + Ribbon + Hystrix from the pre-Kubernetes era.

**Definition:** Sidecar proxy attached to every pod that handles discovery, LB, retries, mTLS, and observability transparently.
**Trade-offs:** Most powerful (L7 policies, mTLS for free, rich telemetry), but heaviest — adds a proxy per pod, control plane to run, and a learning curve.
**Application Example:** Istio in a 200-service cluster providing automatic mTLS and a global view of request rates and error rates.
Health checks — the unsung hero
children.note: A registry is only useful if it knows what's actually alive.

children.title:
**Definition:** Health check that asks "is this process running?" — failure triggers a restart.
**Trade-offs:** Recovers from deadlocks and unrecoverable states, but a too-aggressive liveness check during a slow boot can cause restart loops.
**Application Example:** A K8s `livenessProbe` hitting `/healthz` every 10s; three failures in a row → kubelet restarts the container.

**Definition:** Health check that asks "is this process ready to serve?" — failure removes it from the pool but does **not** restart it.
**Trade-offs:** Lets slow-starting or temporarily-overloaded instances drain traffic without being killed, but requires careful design to avoid flapping.
**Application Example:** A `readinessProbe` returning 503 until DB connections are warm and migrations finish; the LB skips the pod until it's truly ready.

**Definition:** Service Discovery is the foundation that makes horizontal scaling, zero-downtime deploys, and self-healing actually work.
**Trade-offs:** Without it every deploy or autoscale event breaks callers; with it the cluster behaves as one elastic system.
**Application Example:** A 50-service company shipping 200 deploys/day relies on discovery + health checks so callers never notice the rolling restarts.

**Definition:** Single front-door service that handles auth, rate-limiting, routing, and aggregation for downstream microservices.
**Trade-offs:** Centralizes cross-cutting concerns and shields clients from internal topology, but is itself a critical hop and can become a bottleneck.
**Application Example:** Kong / AWS API Gateway terminating TLS, validating JWTs, and routing `/orders/*` to the orders service and `/billing/*` to billing.
Resilience and Fault Tolerance
children.note: Prevent local failures from cascading.

children.title:
**Definition:** Wrapper around a remote call that "opens" after repeated failures and short-circuits subsequent calls until the dependency recovers.
**Trade-offs:** Stops cascading failure from an overloaded dependency, but a too-sensitive breaker amplifies brief blips into outages.
**Application Example:** Resilience4j circuit breaker around the payments API — opens after 5 failures in 10s and fails-fast for 30s before a half-open probe.

**Definition:** On transient failure, retry the call with exponentially-growing delays plus jitter.
**Trade-offs:** Recovers from blips automatically, but without backoff/jitter and a cap, retries amplify load on a struggling dependency (retry storms).
**Application Example:** AWS SDK's default retry policy: 3 attempts with `2^n × 100ms` plus full jitter and a 20-second cap.

**Definition:** Isolate resources (thread pools, connections) per dependency so one slow dependency can't drown the rest.
**Trade-offs:** Contains failures and prevents thread starvation, but partitioned resources mean lower peak utilization.
**Application Example:** Two HTTP client thread pools — 30 threads for Stripe, 30 for the search service — so a Stripe slowdown can't exhaust the pool used for search.

**Definition:** Cap on how long any RPC, query, or task will block before the caller gives up.
**Trade-offs:** Prevents indefinite resource hold-ups and unblocks callers, but too-short timeouts waste good results and trigger unnecessary retries.
**Application Example:** A 2-second connect timeout and 5-second read timeout on every outbound HTTP call, with timeouts shorter than the upstream caller's own.

**Definition:** Property that the same operation can be safely repeated and produce the same result.
**Trade-offs:** Makes retries safe and at-least-once delivery survivable, but requires explicit idempotency keys or natural uniqueness, increasing design effort.
**Application Example:** A `POST /payments` accepting an `Idempotency-Key` header so a retry after a network blip doesn't double-charge the customer.
Object Storage and CDNs
children.note: When files don't belong in a database.

children.title:
**Definition:** Service that stores arbitrarily-large opaque "objects" (files) addressed by key, with multiple-copy durability.
**Trade-offs:** Cheap, virtually unlimited, and 11-nines durable, but high per-request latency vs. local disk and no in-place mutation.
**Application Example:** AWS S3 holding user uploads, ML training data, and DB backups — accessed by `s3://bucket/key` URLs.

**Definition:** Distributed cache for static assets and media, served from PoPs close to end users.
**Trade-offs:** Massive latency wins and origin offload, but cache invalidation gets harder farther from origin and costs scale with bandwidth.
**Application Example:** Cloudflare in front of an S3 bucket — JS, CSS, images, and HLS video segments served from ~300 PoPs with `max-age=1y, immutable`.

**Definition:** Decision rule — large media, backups, logs, archives, ML datasets belong in object storage, not a database.
**Trade-offs:** Storing blobs in a DB makes them slow and 100× more expensive to host and back up; storing them in S3 keeps the DB lean.
**Application Example:** Storing user-uploaded videos in S3 and only keeping `s3_key` + metadata in PostgreSQL.
Observability and Monitoring
children.note: You can only fix what you can see.

children.title: Metrics
children.note: Numerical, time-stamped measurements. Cheap to store and aggregate, perfect for dashboards and alerts. Tools: **Prometheus**, Datadog, CloudWatch. Tie them to **SLIs/SLOs** so you alert on user-facing pain, not internal noise.

children.title: RED (for request-driven services)
children.note: Best general framework for HTTP/RPC services.

children.title:
**Definition:** Requests per second, broken down by endpoint, method, and status.
**Trade-offs:** The denominator that lets you compute error rate and a leading capacity-planning signal, but high cardinality (per-endpoint × per-status) costs storage.
**Application Example:** A Prometheus counter `http_requests_total{route, status}` scraped every 15s, queried as `rate(... [5m])`.

**Definition:** Rate of failed requests — explicit (5xx, exceptions) and implicit (200 OK with wrong content).
**Trade-offs:** The most user-impacting RED signal, but you must alert on the **ratio** (errors / total) so it scales with traffic.
**Application Example:** An alert that fires when `rate(http_5xx[5m]) / rate(http_total[5m]) > 0.01` for 5 minutes.

**Definition:** Latency distribution per endpoint, tracked at p50/p95/p99 (never the mean).
**Trade-offs:** Reveals tail-latency problems that real users feel, but histogram metrics are expensive to store and aggregate.
**Application Example:** Prometheus histogram `http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{route}` queried via `histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(...))`.
USE (for resources / hardware)
children.note: Best for CPUs, disks, queues, connection pools.

children.title:
**Definition:** Fraction of time a resource is busy (CPU, disk, NIC).
**Trade-offs:** Easy to interpret, but a 100%-utilized resource isn't always a problem — and a 30% one can still be saturated if its queue is full.
**Application Example:** Linux `node_cpu_seconds_total` used to compute per-core CPU utilization at 1-minute resolution.

**Definition:** Backlog or queue depth waiting on a resource — the leading signal before utilization hits 100%.
**Trade-offs:** Catches problems earlier than utilization, but per-resource saturation metrics often need the OS/runtime to expose them.
**Application Example:** Linux `node_load1` over `node_cpu_count` (load average / cores), or a thread-pool's `queue.size` counter.

**Definition:** Resource-level error counts — disk I/O errors, dropped packets, OOM kills, retransmits.
**Trade-offs:** Often catches hardware/OS issues that RED-style errors miss, but tools and locations are scattered (dmesg, `node_exporter`, NIC counters).
**Application Example:** An alert on `node_disk_io_now > 0 and node_disk_io_errors_total > 0` to catch a failing SSD before it takes the DB down.
Four Golden Signals (Google SRE)
children.note: Almost the same as RED + saturation — what to watch on every service.

children.title:
**Definition:** How long a request takes to be served, tracked separately for success and failure.
**Trade-offs:** Failures can be artificially fast (instant 500s) and skew the mean — always slice success vs. error and look at percentiles.
**Application Example:** Splitting `http_request_duration_seconds` by `status="2xx"` vs. `status="5xx"` so a flood of fast 500s doesn't make the p99 look healthy.

**Definition:** Demand on the system — requests/sec, transactions/sec, messages/sec.
**Trade-offs:** The denominator for ratios and the input to capacity planning, but choosing the right unit (requests vs. bytes vs. transactions) matters.
**Application Example:** `rate(http_requests_total[5m])` for an HTTP service or `rate(kafka_messages_consumed_total[5m])` for a streaming worker.

**Definition:** Rate of failed requests — alert on the ratio so the threshold scales with traffic.
**Trade-offs:** Thresholds tuned at one traffic level break at another; ratios are robust but mask absolute volume during low traffic.
**Application Example:** An SLO of 99.9% success → alert when `errors / total > 0.001` over a 30-minute rolling window.

**Definition:** How "full" the service is — thread-pool depth, queue length, GC pressure, CPU near 100%.
**Trade-offs:** Best leading indicator of overload, but each runtime exposes a different metric — there's no single number.
**Application Example:** Tomcat's `tomcat_threadpool_currentthreadsbusy / max_threads > 0.8` as a saturation alert before requests start queueing.

**Definition:** User vs. system CPU % plus 1/5/15-minute load averages.
**Trade-offs:** Sustained `load > #cores` indicates contention; sudden spikes often correlate with bad releases, but in containers cgroup quotas make raw % misleading.
**Application Example:** `1 - rate(node_cpu_seconds_total{mode="idle"}[5m])` per core, alerting on > 80% sustained.

**Definition:** RSS, heap, available memory, and swap activity.
**Trade-offs:** Swapping kills performance long before OOM, so monitor `swap_in/out` even if total memory looks fine; in containers, watch the cgroup limit.
**Application Example:** Alert when `container_memory_working_set_bytes / spec.memory.limit > 0.9` for 5 minutes — the OOMKiller is imminent.

**Definition:** I/O operations/sec, per-op latency, throughput, queue depth, and free space.
**Trade-offs:** Filling disks crash log writers, brokers, and DBs without warning; alert at 80% used and page at 90%.
**Application Example:** `node_filesystem_avail_bytes / node_filesystem_size_bytes < 0.2` alert on every host, plus a `disk_io_time_seconds_total` saturation alert.

**Definition:** Bytes in/out, packet errors, TCP retransmits, connection counts.
**Trade-offs:** TCP retransmits > 1% indicates a real network problem; raw throughput tells you little without context.
**Application Example:** `rate(node_netstat_Tcp_RetransSegs[5m]) / rate(node_netstat_Tcp_OutSegs[5m]) > 0.01` to catch a flaky link.

**Definition:** Time spent in garbage collection plus heap-after-GC trends.
**Trade-offs:** GC > 5% of CPU = mis-tuned heap; heap-after-GC steadily growing = leak.
**Application Example:** `jvm_gc_pause_seconds_sum / process_cpu_seconds_total > 0.05` alert, plus a panel of `jvm_memory_used_bytes{area="heap"}` after each Old-gen GC.

**Definition:** Active and idle counts of OS threads, JVM threads, or Go goroutines.
**Trade-offs:** Steadily-growing counts = leak; sudden plateau at the OS limit = the cause of mysterious "hangs".
**Application Example:** Go `go_goroutines` panel; an alert when it exceeds 10,000 because the app rarely needs more than 1,000.
DB connection pool: active / idle / waiting

**Definition:** How many DB connections are in use, available, and how many requests are blocked waiting for one.
**Trade-offs:** `waiting > 0` is the first symptom of every DB-induced outage — either the pool is too small or downstream queries are too slow.
**Application Example:** HikariCP `hikaricp_pending_threads > 0` alert; pair with a slow-query log to find the culprit.

**Definition:** `hits / (hits + misses)` for each cache layer.
**Trade-offs:** A drop usually means cache thrash, key churn, or a deploy that changed query keys; measure per-cache (Redis vs. application vs. CDN).
**Application Example:** Redis `keyspace_hits / (keyspace_hits + keyspace_misses)` panel; a 95% → 70% drop after a deploy is a smoking gun.
Business / product metrics
children.note: The ones the business actually cares about — and the strongest signal that something is broken.

children.title:
**Definition:** Real-user activity rates — the highest-fidelity signal something is broken.
**Trade-offs:** A 30% drop in checkouts beats any infrastructure metric — your stack might be green and the business still bleeding.
**Application Example:** A "Business KPIs" Grafana board with `rate(checkout_completed_total[5m])` and an alert when it drops > 30% from the same hour last week.

**Definition:** Money in / money attempted, fluctuating with provider issues, fraud rules, and expired cards.
**Trade-offs:** Alert on the **rate of decline**, not absolute number — baseline varies by hour and country.
**Application Example:** An anomaly alert on `payment_success_total / payment_attempt_total` using a 2-week seasonality model.

**Definition:** DAU/WAU/MAU and per-stage drop-off through the funnel (visit → cart → pay → confirm).
**Trade-offs:** Pinpoints where users abandon — UX or perf regressions show up here before any error count moves.
**Application Example:** An Amplitude funnel where the visit → cart step drops 8% after a deploy that slowed the product page.

**Definition:** Page on user-facing pain (latency, error rate, business KPI) rather than internal resource numbers.
**Trade-offs:** Cause-based alerts (CPU 80%) flap and create alert fatigue without a real impact; symptom-based alerts page on what actually matters.
**Application Example:** Pager rule "checkout error rate > 1% for 5 minutes" instead of "API host CPU > 80%".

**Definition:** Track p50/p95/p99 of latency, never the mean.
**Trade-offs:** Means hide the long tail that hurts real users; percentiles cost more to store and aggregate but reflect actual UX.
**Application Example:** A Prometheus histogram with buckets at 5/25/50/100/250/500/1000ms and a `histogram_quantile(0.99, ...)` panel.

**Definition:** Discipline of choosing useful dimensions (service, endpoint, version, region) without explosive cardinality.
**Trade-offs:** Each unique label combination is a separate time-series — too many destroy your metrics backend's storage and queries.
**Application Example:** Labels `{service, endpoint, status}` are fine; adding `user_id` would create millions of series and crash Prometheus.

**Definition:** Aggregated, structured, searchable logs from all services in one system.
**Trade-offs:** Essential for incident investigation, but log volume balloons and structured-logging discipline must be enforced.
**Application Example:** Fluent Bit shipping JSON logs from every pod into Loki; `service=billing AND level=ERROR AND trace_id=abc123` searches.

**Definition:** Records the path of one request across all services, with per-hop spans and latencies.
**Trade-offs:** Pinpoints the slow service across a 20-hop request, but cost forces sampling and instrumentation must be propagated through every service.
**Application Example:** OpenTelemetry SDK in every service exporting to Jaeger; one slow checkout shows the 2.3s spent in the recommendation service.

**Definition:** Automated paging on conditions that warrant a human waking up.
**Trade-offs:** Alert on too few things and you miss outages; alert on too many and people stop reading the pages.
**Application Example:** PagerDuty rule with one symptom-based alert per SLO, runbook URL, and a 5-minute "for" window to filter blips.
Decomposition Strategies (Monolith → Microservices)
children.note: How to evolve safely without a Big-Bang rewrite.

children.title:
**Definition:** Cut services along the existing technical modules of the monolith — auth, email, reporting.
**Trade-offs:** Easy to spot from the codebase, but those modules were drawn for developers, not the business — services share data and chat constantly, recreating the monolith over the network.
**Application Example:** A team splitting their Rails app into `auth-service`, `email-service`, `reporting-service` and discovering each one calls the others on every request.
Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
children.note: Cut along **business capabilities** instead of code modules. Each service owns a **Bounded Context** with its own model and language; teams talk to domain experts to find these seams. Harder to discover but produces services that are genuinely independent.

children.title:
**Definition:** Where the boundary is discovered — the current code structure (Functionality) vs. the business model (DDD).
**Trade-offs:** Code-first is faster to start; business-first takes more effort up front but produces durable boundaries.
**Application Example:** Functionality-first: read the repo and split on existing folders. DDD-first: hold an event-storming workshop with domain experts to map what actually happens in the business.

**Definition:** What defines the seam — technical layers (Functionality) vs. **Bounded Contexts** (DDD).
**Trade-offs:** Technical layers cross-cut concerns; bounded contexts let the same word ("Customer") mean different things in Sales vs. Shipping.
**Application Example:** A "Customer" in Sales has prospects and quotes; in Shipping it's an address and SLA. DDD models them as two distinct types in two services; Functionality forces one shared one.

**Definition:** Whether services share data — Functionality often shares one DB / DTOs (invisible coupling); DDD has each context own its data.
**Trade-offs:** Sharing is cheaper short-term but couples services so they must deploy together; ownership is more work but enables independent change.
**Application Example:** DDD: Sales owns its DB and emits `CustomerSignedUp` events; Shipping subscribes and projects its own view. No shared schema, no shared DB.

**Definition:** How the team talks — developer terms (Functionality) vs. **Ubiquitous Language** (DDD) shared with the business.
**Trade-offs:** Ubiquitous Language reduces translation bugs between business and engineering, but takes effort to maintain consistency.
**Application Example:** Code, docs, and meetings all use "Quote", "Order", "Shipment" instead of `OrderEntity`, `OrderProcessor`, `ShipmentDTO` — same terms as the salesperson uses.

**Definition:** The system you end up with — Functionality often produces a *distributed monolith*; DDD produces autonomous services.
**Trade-offs:** A distributed monolith has microservices' costs without the benefits; DDD aligns services to teams and Conway's Law works for you.
**Application Example:** Functionality outcome: 12 services that must deploy in lock-step. DDD outcome: 12 services where any team can ship to prod independently on any given day.

**Definition:** Up-front investment vs. long-term payoff — DDD is slower to start, faster to evolve.
**Trade-offs:** DDD's workshops, event storming, and modeling sessions feel like overhead, but they pay back when teams ship independently and boundaries don't need rewriting.
**Application Example:** A 3-week Event Storming + bounded-context-mapping kickoff before writing code, vs. starting to code on day one with code-first splits.

**Definition:** Migration pattern that routes slices of traffic to new services while gradually retiring the old paths.
**Trade-offs:** Avoids Big-Bang rewrites and keeps the system always-shippable, but the migration period is long and runs two stacks in parallel.
**Application Example:** An nginx routing rule that sends `/checkout/*` to a new Go service and everything else to the legacy Rails monolith — the old code shrinks each sprint.

**Definition:** Splitting the shared monolith DB so each service owns its data — almost always the hardest step.
**Trade-offs:** Eliminates coupling at the data layer (the deepest kind), but breaks foreign keys, joins, and atomic transactions across what used to be one DB.
**Application Example:** Extracting the `orders.*` tables into a separate Postgres for the Orders service, with the rest of the monolith reading via API or events instead of a JOIN.
Security
children.note: Defending the system across the auth, network, data, code, and operational planes.

children.title: Authentication
children.note: Proving **who** the caller is.

children.title:
**Definition:** Stateful auth where the server stores a session record and hands the client an opaque session ID inside an HTTP-only cookie.
**Trade-offs:** Trivial to revoke (delete the row) and naturally CSRF-protected with `SameSite=Lax`, but every API node needs access to the session store, which becomes a hot dependency.
**Application Example:** A monolithic web app keeping sessions in Redis with a 24-hour TTL; signing out drops the key and the next request 401s.

**Definition:** Self-contained signed JSON token (`header.payload.signature`) carrying the user's identity and claims; verified via signature without hitting a session store.
**Trade-offs:** Stateless and great for microservices, but **revocation is hard** — you can't "log out" until the token expires, which forces short TTLs and refresh tokens.
**Application Example:** An API gateway validating an RS256-signed access token in `Authorization: Bearer …` on every call without a DB lookup.

**Definition:** OAuth 2.0 is a delegated **authorization** framework; OpenID Connect (OIDC) layers **identity** (an `id_token`) on top.
**Trade-offs:** Standardised flows (authorization code + PKCE for SPAs/mobile, client credentials for service-to-service), but the spec is broad and easy to mis-implement — the wrong flow leaks tokens.
**Application Example:** "Sign in with Google" using OIDC's authorization code + PKCE flow, returning an `id_token` plus an `access_token` for downstream APIs.

**Definition:** Long-lived static secrets a client sends in a header (e.g. `X-API-Key`) to identify itself.
**Trade-offs:** Dead-simple to issue and rotate, but easy to leak (logs, repos, screenshots) and offer no user identity — only "this caller knows the secret".
**Application Example:** A SaaS platform issuing per-tenant API keys for backend integrations, scoped to a project and revocable from a dashboard.

**Definition:** Require a second factor beyond the password — something the user **has** (TOTP app, security key) or **is** (biometric).
**Trade-offs:** Dramatically reduces phishing/credential-stuffing risk, but adds friction and recovery flows to design carefully.
**Application Example:** GitHub requiring a TOTP code or WebAuthn passkey on every new device login for accounts contributing to popular open-source repos.

**Definition:** Enterprise-grade auth where a central Identity Provider (Okta, Azure AD, Google) issues assertions/tokens to multiple downstream apps.
**Trade-offs:** One login across many tools and centralised offboarding, but adds an integration tax and hard-couples uptime to the IdP.
**Application Example:** An employee logging into Slack, Notion, and Datadog via Okta SAML — disabling the user in Okta cuts access everywhere.

**Definition:** Store an **adaptive** hash (bcrypt / argon2 / scrypt) of the password, not the password itself; cost is tuned so a verify takes ~100ms.
**Trade-offs:** Resilient to GPU/ASIC cracking even after a DB leak, at the cost of CPU/memory per login — must tune work factor per hardware.
**Application Example:** User signup runs `argon2id(password, salt, t=3, m=64MB, p=1)` and stores the encoded hash; login re-runs the same parameters to compare.
Authorization
children.note: Deciding **what** the caller is allowed to do.

children.title:
**Definition:** Role-Based Access Control — assign users to **roles** (admin, editor, viewer) and grant permissions to roles.
**Trade-offs:** Easy to reason about and audit at small scale, but explodes ("role explosion") when permissions need to vary by resource, owner, or attribute.
**Application Example:** A SaaS app where `admin` can create projects, `editor` can write within their project, and `viewer` can only read — defined in a permissions table per role.

**Definition:** Attribute-Based Access Control — decisions evaluate **attributes** of the user, resource, action, and environment via policies.
**Trade-offs:** Far more expressive than RBAC and avoids role explosion, but harder to reason about and audit — "who can do X?" is no longer a simple lookup.
**Application Example:** A policy: `allow if user.department == resource.department AND time.hour BETWEEN 9 AND 18`, evaluated by an OPA sidecar on each request.

**Definition:** Relationship-Based Access Control — model permissions as graphs of relationships (`user X is owner of folder Y`, `folder Y is parent of file Z`).
**Trade-offs:** Naturally fits social/document/folder hierarchies and scales to billions of relationships, but needs a dedicated service and careful schema design.
**Application Example:** Google Drive sharing modeled in Zanzibar: "can_view(file)" walks `editor` → `parent_folder` → `editor` relationships transitively.

**Definition:** Externalised decision engines that take `(subject, action, resource, context)` and return allow/deny based on declarative policies.
**Trade-offs:** Decouple policy from code so policies can be reviewed and updated independently, but add a network hop and a new component to operate.
**Application Example:** An Envoy sidecar consulting OPA on every gRPC call against a Rego policy bundle versioned in Git and pushed via OCI registry.
Server-to-server auth
children.note: Identity for non-human callers — cron jobs, services, scripts, deploy pipelines.

children.title:
**Definition:** Mutual TLS — both client AND server present X.509 certificates so each side cryptographically authenticates the other.
**Trade-offs:** Strongest form of pinned identity for service-to-service traffic and impossible to phish, but requires cert lifecycle automation (issuance, rotation, revocation).
**Application Example:** Service-mesh sidecars (Istio, Linkerd) automatically issuing short-lived per-pod certs from an internal CA so all in-cluster traffic is mTLS-encrypted and -authenticated.

**Definition:** OAuth 2.0 flow for backend-to-backend auth: the client exchanges a `client_id`/`client_secret` for a short-lived access token.
**Trade-offs:** Standardised, scope-aware, and integrates with existing IdPs, but secret distribution is your problem and short tokens require frequent refreshes.
**Application Example:** A nightly batch job fetching an access token from Auth0 with `grant_type=client_credentials` and using it to call an internal API.
Service accounts (K8s SA, AWS IAM roles, GCP service accounts)

**Definition:** Cloud/platform-native identities attached to a workload — the platform mints short-lived credentials transparently, no static secrets.
**Trade-offs:** Eliminates credential leakage (no secrets to steal) and inherits IAM auditing, but ties the workload tightly to the platform.
**Application Example:** A pod with `serviceAccountName: orders-app` getting an IRSA token that lets it `s3:GetObject` on one specific bucket, without any AWS keys in code or env.

**Definition:** Asymmetric auth where a server stores the user's public key and accepts logins signed with the matching private key.
**Trade-offs:** Phishing-proof and easy to revoke (delete the key from `authorized_keys`), but a leaked private key without a passphrase is full compromise.
**Application Example:** Engineers SSH'ing into bastion hosts using ed25519 keys whose public part is provisioned via configuration management; passwords are disabled.
Encryption
children.note: Protect data in motion and at rest.

children.title:
**Definition:** Encrypts data while it travels over the network, defending against passive sniffing and active tampering.
**Trade-offs:** Universal and battle-tested, but cert lifecycle is its own operational discipline and TLS termination location matters (LB vs. pod).
**Application Example:** An Application Load Balancer terminating TLS with an ACM cert at the edge and re-encrypting to pods over mTLS via the service mesh.

**Definition:** Encrypts data while it sits on disk; the data key encrypts data, and a Key Management Service (KMS) wraps the data key.
**Trade-offs:** Centralises key custody and audit (KMS logs every use), but performance depends on caching the unwrapped key and key rotation needs careful design.
**Application Example:** An S3 bucket with SSE-KMS encryption: each object has a unique data key wrapped by `arn:aws:kms:…:keys/…`, decrypt-on-fetch by IAM-authorised callers.

**Definition:** **Symmetric:** one shared key used for both encrypt and decrypt (AES). **Asymmetric:** a key pair where the public key encrypts and only the private key decrypts (RSA, ECC).
**Trade-offs:** Symmetric is fast but key distribution is the problem; asymmetric solves distribution but is ~1000× slower — real systems combine both.
**Application Example:** TLS handshake uses asymmetric (RSA/ECDHE) to negotiate a shared symmetric session key (AES-GCM), then encrypts the bulk traffic with the symmetric key.

**Definition:** **Hashing** is a one-way function producing a fixed-length fingerprint; **encryption** is reversible with the right key.
**Trade-offs:** Hashes can't be undone — perfect for password verification and integrity checks; encryption is for confidentiality of data you'll need back.
**Application Example:** Storing `argon2id(password)` in the DB (hash) but encrypting users' API keys with KMS (encrypt) so the support team can re-display them.
Secrets management
children.note: Where credentials live and how they get into the workload that needs them.

children.title:
**Definition:** Centralised stores that issue, version, and audit secrets; apps fetch them at runtime instead of reading from env vars or files.
**Trade-offs:** One auditable place for credentials with fine-grained access, but the secrets store becomes a Tier-0 dependency that must be HA.
**Application Example:** A microservice authenticating to Vault via its K8s service account and fetching a database password that rotates every 24 hours.

**Definition:** Periodically replacing credentials so a leaked secret has a short useful life.
**Trade-offs:** Drastically shrinks the blast radius of a leak, but rotation must be automated end-to-end — manual rotation creates outages and gets skipped.
**Application Example:** AWS RDS IAM-database authentication issuing 15-minute tokens, or Vault's database secrets engine creating per-app DB users with TTLs.

**Definition:** CI runners (GitHub Actions, GitLab) mint a short-lived OIDC token at job start; cloud providers exchange it for temporary IAM credentials based on a trust relationship.
**Trade-offs:** Eliminates long-lived static keys in CI (no more `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` repo secret), but requires correct trust-policy setup per repo/branch.
**Application Example:** A GitHub Actions deploy job using `aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials` with `role-to-assume` and trust policy gating on `repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/main`.
OWASP Top 10
children.note: The application-level vulnerability classes every senior engineer should recognise on sight.

children.title:
**Definition:** Untrusted input concatenated into a query/command and executed by the interpreter (DB, shell, LDAP server).
**Trade-offs:** Catastrophic when exploited (full DB read/write or RCE), but trivially preventable with parameterised queries and input validation.
**Application Example:** `SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = '${id}'` allowing `' OR 1=1 --`; the fix is `?`-parameterised queries via the driver.

**Definition:** Flaws in login, session, or credential handling — predictable session IDs, password reset abuse, missing rate limits, no MFA.
**Trade-offs:** Often invisible until exploited and devastating because it grants account-level access; mitigated by adopting battle-tested IdPs over rolling your own.
**Application Example:** An app that returns 200 OK for valid users and 404 for unknown users on `POST /login`, allowing username enumeration before brute-force.

**Definition:** Cross-Site Scripting — attacker-supplied input rendered into a victim's browser as executable JS, hijacking session/UI in their context.
**Trade-offs:** Common in template/HTML output and dangerous because it bypasses same-origin protections, but a strict CSP + framework auto-escaping kills 99% of cases.
**Application Example:** A comment field saving `` and rendering it raw on the page; the fix is contextual escaping + `Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self'`.

**Definition:** Cross-Site Request Forgery — a malicious site causes a logged-in user's browser to issue authenticated state-changing requests to your app.
**Trade-offs:** Devastating for cookie-auth apps, neutralised by `SameSite=Lax/Strict` cookies plus an anti-CSRF token on state-changing endpoints.
**Application Example:** An attacker page submitting `
`; the bank rejects the request because the session cookie is `SameSite=Strict`.

**Definition:** Server-Side Request Forgery — the server is tricked into making outbound requests to attacker-chosen URLs (often internal: `169.254.169.254` cloud metadata).
**Trade-offs:** High-impact in cloud environments because metadata endpoints leak credentials, but mitigated by allow-listing outbound destinations and blocking link-local IPs.
**Application Example:** An image-resize service fetching `?url=http://169.254.169.254/…` and returning IMDS-stolen IAM credentials; the fix is a strict outbound URL allow-list and IMDSv2.
Threat modeling
children.note: Structured way to enumerate threats before they become incidents.

children.title:
**Definition:** Acronym for **S**poofing, **T**ampering, **R**epudiation, **I**nformation disclosure, **D**enial of service, **E**levation of privilege — a checklist of threat categories per data-flow boundary.
**Trade-offs:** Forces a systematic look at trust boundaries early in design, but only as good as the model — high-level architectures miss low-level bugs.
**Application Example:** A whiteboard data-flow diagram of a checkout flow with each arrow annotated with applicable STRIDE categories and corresponding mitigations.

**Definition:** Enumerate every place an attacker could interact with the system — endpoints, ports, dependencies, supply chain — and quantify risk.
**Trade-offs:** Drives prioritisation ("reduce surface" → fewer exposed endpoints), but the surface drifts every deploy so the analysis must be continuous.
**Application Example:** An ASM tool inventorying all external-facing endpoints across cloud accounts weekly and flagging newly-exposed admin panels or unauthenticated services.
Networking fundamentals
children.note: The TCP/IP stack you have to be fluent in to design distributed systems.

children.title:
**Definition:** Distributed name-resolution system mapping human-readable names (`api.example.com`) to IPs and other records (MX, TXT, SRV).
**Trade-offs:** Hierarchical and aggressively cached for low-latency lookups, but TTLs make changes propagate slowly and DNS is a notorious source of subtle outages.
**Application Example:** A multi-region deployment using Route 53 latency-based routing to send each user's `api.example.com` query to the nearest healthy regional ALB, with a 60-second TTL.

**Definition:** **TCP:** reliable, ordered, connection-oriented stream with congestion control; **UDP:** best-effort, unordered, connectionless datagrams.
**Trade-offs:** TCP is the default for correctness, but its retransmits and head-of-line blocking are bad for real-time and bursty traffic — UDP wins when you'd rather drop than wait.
**Application Example:** HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 ride TCP; HTTP/3, QUIC, DNS, and most VoIP/video conferencing ride UDP for the latency win.

**Definition:** Transport Layer Security — encrypted, authenticated channel over TCP using certificates and a session-key handshake.
**Trade-offs:** Defends against passive sniffing and active MITM, but cert lifecycle and protocol versions are a constant source of operational pain.
**Application Example:** A modern web service serving HTTPS using TLS 1.3 (one-RTT handshake), an ECDSA cert from Let's Encrypt rotated by `cert-manager`, and ALPN-negotiated HTTP/2.

**Definition:** **HTTP/1.1:** plain-text, one request per connection; **HTTP/2:** binary, multiplexed over one TCP connection (HPACK header compression); **HTTP/3:** HTTP/2 semantics over QUIC (UDP).
**Trade-offs:** Each version trades complexity for fewer round-trips and better head-of-line behaviour — HTTP/3 finally fixes TCP HoL blocking but isn't supported everywhere.
**Application Example:** An API serving HTTP/2 between LB and origin (multiplexed gRPC), with HTTP/3 enabled at the edge for clients on mobile networks where QUIC's recovery is fastest.

**Definition:** Bidirectional or push-style protocols for live data: **WebSockets** (full-duplex over TCP), **SSE** (server-push over HTTP), **long-polling** (held-open HTTP request), **WebRTC** (P2P audio/video/data over UDP).
**Trade-offs:** Each fits a different latency/complexity sweet spot — WebSockets for chat and collaboration, SSE for one-way streams (notifications), WebRTC for media.
**Application Example:** A collaborative editor using WebSockets for cursor positions and edits, SSE for low-priority notifications, and WebRTC for an in-app video call between collaborators.
Testing strategies
children.note: Layered confidence — fast cheap tests for most code, slow expensive tests for the riskiest paths.

children.title:
**Definition:** Cohn's hierarchy: lots of fast unit tests → fewer integration tests → very few end-to-end tests, with cost and flakiness rising as you go up.
**Trade-offs:** Optimises feedback time by catching most bugs at the cheapest layer, but only works if the layers below are actually exercising the right behaviour.
**Application Example:** A microservice with 5,000 unit tests (run in 8s), 200 integration tests against a real Postgres in CI (3 min), and 20 critical-path e2e tests on a staging URL (10 min).

**Definition:** Tests one function/class in isolation, mocking or stubbing collaborators.
**Trade-offs:** Fast and pinpoint failures, but heavy mocking can lock the test to the implementation and hide refactoring opportunities.
**Application Example:** A `formatCurrency(amount, locale)` test asserting outputs for 12 locale combinations, finishing in milliseconds with no I/O.

**Definition:** Tests several real components together — service + DB, service + queue — with as little mocking as possible.
**Trade-offs:** Catches contract mismatches and SQL/migration bugs that unit tests miss, at the cost of slower runs and more flake from environment setup.
**Application Example:** Spinning up Postgres via Testcontainers and running the order-creation HTTP handler against the real schema, asserting both response and resulting rows.

**Definition:** Consumer-driven contracts where the client publishes its expectations as a contract and the provider verifies against it in CI.
**Trade-offs:** Catches breaking API changes before deploy without a flaky e2e environment, but requires both teams to adopt the tooling and discipline.
**Application Example:** Pact: the `web` consumer publishes its expected `/orders` contract; the `orders-service` CI replays it and fails the build if the response shape regresses.

**Definition:** Drive the full stack — real browser, real backend, real DB — to verify user flows.
**Trade-offs:** The closest test to actual user experience, but slow, flaky, and expensive to maintain — keep the suite small and focused on critical paths.
**Application Example:** A Playwright test running on a staging URL that signs up, creates a project, invites a teammate, and asserts the welcome email arrives.

**Definition:** Synthetic-traffic generation that exercises the system at expected and peak load to measure latency, throughput, and saturation points.
**Trade-offs:** Surfaces capacity limits and bottlenecks before users do, but realistic load scenarios (think distributions, not RPS averages) are hard to design.
**Application Example:** A k6 script ramping to 5,000 RPS with realistic user-journey distributions, run pre-Black-Friday against a production-sized staging environment.

**Definition:** Deliberately inject faults — kill pods, drop packets, throttle CPU — in production-like environments to verify the system survives.
**Trade-offs:** Builds genuine confidence in resilience, but requires solid observability and a culture that tolerates the surprises chaos uncovers.
**Application Example:** Netflix Chaos Monkey randomly terminating EC2 instances during business hours; Gremlin / Litmus injecting CPU/network faults into K8s clusters on a schedule.

**Definition:** Stand-ins for real collaborators in tests: **mock** (verifies calls), **stub** (returns canned responses), **fake** (working in-memory implementation), **spy** (records calls).
**Trade-offs:** Critical for fast deterministic tests, but overuse couples tests to implementation — refactors go red despite behaviour being unchanged.
**Application Example:** Replacing the SMTP client with a fake that records every send-attempt in memory; the test asserts a `WelcomeEmail` was queued without touching real email infrastructure.

**Definition:** Frameworks generate random inputs and shrink failing cases; you assert **invariants** ("output is always sorted", "encode→decode is identity") instead of fixed examples.
**Trade-offs:** Finds bugs example-based tests miss (edge cases at type boundaries), but requires expressing invariants — not always obvious for business logic.
**Application Example:** Hypothesis (Python) or fast-check (TS) generating arbitrary lists and asserting `sorted(reverse(sorted(xs))) == sorted(xs)` to find subtle comparator bugs.

**Definition:** A tool mutates the production code (flips `>` to `<`, removes statements) and re-runs the test suite; surviving mutants reveal **gaps** in the tests.
**Trade-offs:** Measures test **quality**, not just coverage, but slow (re-runs the suite hundreds of times) and noisy (some mutants are equivalent).
**Application Example:** Stryker (JS/TS) running once per release in CI; surviving mutants in the payment-engine module trigger a quality-gate failure until tests are tightened.
API design
children.note: Designing public-facing surfaces that survive years of clients and change.

children.title:
**Definition:** Four-level model: **L0** RPC over HTTP, **L1** resources, **L2** HTTP verbs/status codes, **L3** hypermedia (HATEOAS).
**Trade-offs:** Higher levels are more discoverable and cacheable, but most production APIs sit at L2 — L3 adoption is rare in practice.
**Application Example:** A REST API exposing `/orders/{id}` (resource), using `POST /orders` and `GET /orders/{id}` correctly with `201 Created` and `Location` headers (L2).

**Definition:** Strategy for evolving an API without breaking existing clients — URL path (`/v1/`), header (`Accept: application/vnd.api.v2+json`), or content negotiation.
**Trade-offs:** URL versioning is most discoverable and trivial to route; header versioning keeps URLs stable but is invisible to caches and humans.
**Application Example:** Stripe pinning each merchant to a versioned API release and rolling out new versions with full changelogs and migration guides.

**Definition:** Client-supplied unique key (header `Idempotency-Key`) that the server uses to deduplicate retried writes.
**Trade-offs:** Makes at-least-once delivery safe, but the server must persist the key + response for some retention window — extra storage and deduplication logic.
**Application Example:** `POST /payments` with `Idempotency-Key: 8a7…` so a network blip during checkout doesn't double-charge the customer; the server returns the original response on retry.

**Definition:** **Offset:** `?page=2&size=50`; **cursor/keyset:** `?after=ulid…&size=50`; **limit/offset on indexed columns** as a middle ground.
**Trade-offs:** Offset is intuitive but breaks at large offsets (slow scans, items shifting); cursor/keyset is stable and fast but harder to jump to arbitrary pages.
**Application Example:** A timeline endpoint paginating with `?after_id=…&limit=50` so newly-inserted items don't shift older items into duplicated/skipped pages.

**Definition:** Structured error responses, ideally **RFC 7807 Problem Details** — `type`, `title`, `status`, `detail`, `instance` — instead of free-form strings.
**Trade-offs:** Lets clients reliably handle errors and translate them, but only valuable if the team disciplines itself to use the same structure everywhere.
**Application Example:** Returning `application/problem+json` with `{ "type": "...", "title": "Insufficient funds", "status": 422, "balance": 0.50 }` from a payments API.

**Definition:** Standard response headers (`X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`) plus `Retry-After` on `429`.
**Trade-offs:** Lets well-behaved clients self-throttle without retry storms, but headers are advisory — the server must still enforce.
**Application Example:** An API returning `429 Too Many Requests` with `Retry-After: 30` and `X-RateLimit-Reset: 1714…`; SDKs back off automatically using these headers.

**Definition:** Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State — responses embed links describing what actions the client can take next.
**Trade-offs:** Self-describing and theoretically decouples client from URLs, but adoption is rare because most clients hard-code routes anyway.
**Application Example:** An order response embedding `{ "_links": { "cancel": { "href": "/orders/123/cancel" }, "refund": { "href": "…" } } }` so clients only follow links they're authorised for.

**Definition:** Define the wire contract in a schema (OpenAPI for REST, Protobuf for gRPC) and generate clients/servers/docs from it.
**Trade-offs:** Single source of truth for the API and codegen across many languages, at the cost of a schema-as-code workflow that some teams find heavy.
**Application Example:** An internal API defined in `.proto` files, generating Go and TypeScript clients via `buf generate`; SDK changes are driven by schema PRs.

**Definition:** Evolve the API by **adding** never **removing** — new optional fields, parallel endpoints, deprecation periods.
**Trade-offs:** Lets old clients keep working while new clients adopt new shapes; the cost is supporting more variants and a discipline of additive change.
**Application Example:** Adding `currency` as an optional field on a `Money` object with a default; older clients ignore it, newer clients require it — old responses still parse.

**Definition:** Cross-cutting concerns — auth, rate-limit, routing, request shaping — handled at a single edge component instead of in every service.
**Trade-offs:** Centralises a lot of policy and shields services from clients, but is itself a critical hop and can fragment if every team owns its own gateway.
**Application Example:** Kong / AWS API Gateway terminating TLS, validating JWTs, and routing `/orders/*` to the orders service — services receive only authenticated, rate-limited traffic.
Golang
children.note: Senior-level Go interview classics — concurrency, the runtime, idioms, and the famous gotchas.

children.title: Concurrency
children.note: Go's CSP-inspired model: lightweight goroutines coordinated through typed channels, with `select` as the multi-way switch. Motto: *"Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating."*

children.title:
**Definition:** Lightweight user-space threads scheduled by the Go runtime onto a small pool of OS threads (sized by `GOMAXPROCS`). Spawned by putting the keyword `go` in front of any function call.
**Trade-offs:** Tiny initial stack (~2 KB) that grows on demand makes 100k+ concurrent goroutines cheap, but the runtime exposes no per-goroutine ID, no thread-locals, and no priority — all coordination must go through channels or `sync` primitives, and a leaked goroutine has no built-in cap.
**Application Example:** `net/http` handles every incoming request in its own goroutine for free, so a 10k-RPS service can have tens of thousands of in-flight goroutines without strain — your code just writes the handler as if it were single-threaded.

**Definition:** Typed first-class pipes that pass values between goroutines safely. `make(chan T)` is **unbuffered** — a synchronous handoff where send and receive rendezvous. `make(chan T, N)` is **buffered** — sends succeed without a reader as long as the buffer has room.
**Trade-offs:** Forces explicit communication so the value being passed needs no extra mutex, but every channel op is a runtime call — for very hot paths a `sync.Mutex`-protected slice or a `sync.Pool` can be measurably faster than a channel. Closed channels send forever-unblocking zero values to receivers; sending on a closed channel panics.
**Application Example:** A producer-consumer pipeline: an HTTP handler pushes onto `jobs := make(chan Job, 100)` and a fixed pool of worker goroutines reads from it. Back-pressure is automatic — when the buffer fills the producer blocks until workers drain it.

**Definition:** A multi-way channel switch — `select` blocks until **one** of its cases (a send or receive on different channels) can proceed, then runs that case. An optional `default` branch makes the whole construct non-blocking; a `time.After(d)` arm gives it a timeout.
**Trade-offs:** The only way to wait on multiple channels at once and the foundation of every cancellation/timeout pattern in idiomatic Go, but with N ready cases the runtime picks pseudo-randomly among them — code that depends on case ordering is a bug. A bare `select {}` (no cases) blocks forever, occasionally useful as a "park forever" idiom.
