**Application Example:** A worker reading from `jobs` while honouring cancellation: `select { case job := <-jobs: process(job); case <-ctx.Done(): return }`. Add a `case <-time.After(5*time.Second): handleStall()` arm and you also get a per-iteration deadline.

**Definition:** A goroutine that's been started but never reaches `return` because the channel/operation it's waiting on never completes. Each leaked goroutine pins its stack (~2 KB minimum, growing on demand) plus every variable it captured, so the heap and `runtime.NumGoroutine()` count creep upward forever.
**Trade-offs:** Cheap goroutines (`go f()` in one keystroke) make leaking accidental — there's no compiler warning, no runtime cap, and `pprof goroutine` is the only reliable diagnosis. The runtime only catches **total** deadlocks (when *every* goroutine is blocked); a single leaked goroutine alongside healthy ones is invisible to the scheduler.
**Application Example:** An HTTP handler does `go func() { ch <- expensiveCall() }()` to fan-out work, but the parent returns early on context timeout without ever reading `ch`. The send blocks forever because the channel is unbuffered and there's no reader. Fix: pass `ctx` to the goroutine and use `select { case ch <- result: case <-ctx.Done(): }` so the send aborts cleanly when the request is cancelled.

**Definition:** An unbuffered channel **synchronises** sender and receiver — `ch <- v` blocks until someone is reading, `<-ch` blocks until someone is writing. If both sides aren't ready at the same time, the goroutine hangs.
**Trade-offs:** Perfect for handoffs and coordination (the send completes only once the value has been observed), but a missing reader or sender hangs forever. Go's runtime can detect a *full* deadlock (`fatal error: all goroutines are asleep — deadlock!`), but the much more common case — **one** goroutine stuck while others run normally — produces no error at all, just a leak.
**Application Example:** A worker calls `done <- true` to signal completion, but the dispatcher loop already exited after collecting all results — the worker is stuck forever on the send. Two fixes: (1) use a 1-buffered channel `make(chan bool, 1)` so the send always succeeds, or (2) wrap the send in `select { case done <- true: case <-ctx.Done(): }` so cancellation unblocks it. Rule of thumb: use unbuffered channels for synchronisation, buffered (size 1) for fire-and-forget signals.

**Definition:** Go's memory model defines a `happens-before` relationship; without explicit synchronisation, reads can see stale writes. The `-race` flag instruments code to detect concurrent access.
**Trade-offs:** Race detector catches the worst class of concurrency bugs in test, but adds 5-10× CPU overhead and only flags races that actually fire.
**Application Example:** `go test -race ./…` in CI for every PR, plus `go run -race` on staging during a soak test to surface intermittent races before prod.

**Definition:** Go has a concurrent, tri-color, mark-sweep GC tuned for low pause times (sub-millisecond). The compiler's escape analysis decides whether a value lives on the stack or heap.
**Trade-offs:** Generally invisible and great for productivity, but heap allocations add GC pressure — high-throughput services use `sync.Pool` and watch `go tool pprof` to keep allocations low.
**Application Example:** `go build -gcflags='-m'` to see what escapes to the heap; using `bytes.Buffer` from `sync.Pool` in a hot HTTP handler to avoid per-request allocation.
Struct vs Slice vs Map
children.note: The three core composite types every Go program leans on. Pick by **access pattern**, not familiarity.

children.title:
**Definition:** A composite type that groups **named fields** of (possibly different) typed values into a single value: `type Point struct { X, Y float64 }`. Memory layout is contiguous and field access is a fixed offset — zero overhead vs. C structs.
**Trade-offs:** Best for **fixed, named** sets of fields with strong typing — the compiler catches typos and shape changes. Adding a field anywhere is a recompile, structs aren't iterable without reflection, and field tags (`json:"…"`) are the only way to attach metadata.
**Application Example:** `type User struct { ID int64; Email string; CreatedAt time.Time }` modelling a row of a `users` table — passed by pointer to mutating functions, by value when small and read-only.

**Definition:** A dynamically-sized, ordered sequence of one type, backed by an underlying array. Internally a 3-word header `{pointer, length, capacity}`. `append(s, v)` grows it, doubling capacity when full.
**Trade-offs:** O(1) indexed access and amortised O(1) append, but **slice headers share the underlying array** — `s[1:3]` aliases `s`, so a downstream `append` can clobber the parent (the classic Go gotcha). The zero-value (`nil` slice) is usable for reads and `append`, which is unusual and pleasant.
**Application Example:** `var orders []*Order; orders = append(orders, newOrder)` accumulating results; `for i, o := range orders { ... }` to iterate; `orders[:0]` to reuse the backing array for the next batch without re-allocating.

**Definition:** An unordered hash table of `K → V`, declared `map[K]V`. The zero value of a map is `nil` — reads return the value zero, but writes **panic** until you `make(map[K]V)`.
**Trade-offs:** O(1) average lookup/insert, but **iteration order is randomised by design** (hardens code against accidental ordering dependencies), maps are **not safe for concurrent use** (need `sync.RWMutex` or `sync.Map`), and the underlying buckets grow but never shrink — peak footprint is sticky after deletions.
**Application Example:** A request-scoped cache `attrs := make(map[string]string)`; a counter `counts := map[string]int{}` with `counts[k]++`. For concurrent reads/writes wrap with a `sync.RWMutex`, or use `sync.Map` for read-mostly workloads.

**Definition:** Pick **struct** for fixed shapes with named fields, **slice** for ordered same-typed sequences, **map** for keyed lookups. They compose freely — `[]User`, `map[string][]User`, `struct { items []Item }`.
**Trade-offs:** Each excels at a single access pattern and falls over the others. Reaching for `map[string]any` to model a fixed schema is a smell — you lose type safety and editor autocomplete. Reaching for `[]struct{ Key, Value string }` instead of `map` is a smell when you need O(1) lookup.
**Application Example:** A user profile = `User struct`; their post timeline = `[]Post` (slice — ordered by creation); their followers' IDs = `map[int64]struct{}` (set semantics — O(1) membership check, zero-byte values).

**Definition:** Interfaces are satisfied **structurally** (no `implements` keyword); composition via embedding replaces inheritance.
**Trade-offs:** Tiny interfaces (`io.Reader`, `error`) compose cleanly across the ecosystem, but accidental satisfaction can surprise — and "interface bloat" near the call site is a smell.
**Application Example:** `func process(r io.Reader) error` accepting any `Read`-er — files, network, in-memory buffers — without knowing or caring about the concrete type.
Composition vs Inheritance
children.note: Why Go has no `class extends`. Same code-reuse goals, very different trade-offs from classical OO.

children.title:
**Definition:** Classical OO mechanism where a child class derives from a parent class, automatically getting its fields and methods, and can override behaviour with **virtual dispatch**.
**Trade-offs:** Powerful for natural "is-a" hierarchies and minimal boilerplate at first, but couples derived classes to their parents in fragile ways — changes to the base class ripple outward (fragile-base-class problem), the **diamond problem** kicks in with multiple inheritance, and polymorphic dispatch can hide where behaviour actually lives.
**Application Example:** Java's `class Manager extends Employee` getting `name`, `salary`, and `pay()` for free; overriding `pay()` to add a bonus. Works until `Manager` and `Contractor` need to share something `Employee` doesn't have — and you reach for multiple inheritance, mixins, or traits.

**Definition:** Go's struct embedding inserts another type as an **anonymous field**; the outer struct gains the embedded type's fields and methods via **promotion**, but there's no virtual dispatch — no `super`, no `override`. Interface embedding works the same way for interface composition.
**Trade-offs:** Flat, explicit, and refactor-friendly — every method's location is unambiguous and changes don't cascade unpredictably. The cost: more typing for genuine "is-a" cases, no late-binding polymorphism via base classes (you lean on **interfaces** for that), and method-name collisions you must resolve by hand.
**Application Example:** `type Manager struct { Employee; Reports []*Employee }` — `m.Pay()` calls `Employee.Pay()` for free; if `Manager` defines its own `Pay()`, it shadows the embedded one. For polymorphism, define an `interface{ Pay() float64 }` and pass any struct that implements it.

**Definition:** Go's idiom — **prefer composition (embedding) for code reuse and interfaces for polymorphism**. Reach for inheritance-style modelling only when wrapping an external system that genuinely has a deep hierarchy.
**Trade-offs:** Composition makes intent explicit and refactors safer, but newcomers from OO languages instinctively reach for inheritance and end up fighting the type system. Interfaces look "weakly typed" because of structural satisfaction (any struct with the right methods qualifies, even by accident).
**Application Example:** Designing a new `notifier` package — instead of an abstract base class, define `type Notifier interface { Notify(ctx context.Context, msg string) error }`. Each backend (`EmailNotifier`, `SlackNotifier`, `WebhookNotifier`) is a struct with one method. New backends require no inheritance, just a struct that implements the interface.

**Definition:** Errors are explicit return values. Wrap with `fmt.Errorf("…: %w", err)`; introspect with `errors.Is(err, target)` and `errors.As(err, &target)` for typed checks.
**Trade-offs:** Forces caller awareness at every boundary (no surprise exceptions), but verbose and easy to lose context if you don't wrap.
**Application Example:** `if errors.Is(err, sql.ErrNoRows) { return notFound() }` after wrapping the original DB error with operation context up the stack.

**Definition:** `context.Context` flows as the **first parameter** through every blocking call, carrying cancellation, deadlines, and request-scoped values.
**Trade-offs:** Universal cancellation across goroutines and clean timeout semantics, at the cost of `context.Context` polluting nearly every signature.
**Application Example:** An HTTP handler creating `ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(r.Context(), 2*time.Second)` and passing `ctx` to DB and downstream calls so they all abort if the client disconnects.

**Definition:** Built-in `testing` package: table-driven tests with `t.Run`, microbenchmarks with `b.N`, native fuzzing with `testing.F` since 1.18.
**Trade-offs:** Zero-dependency, fast, and ergonomic for almost everything, but assertion libraries (Testify) are usually added once tests get complex.
**Application Example:** Table-driven `TestParse` with 30 cases via `for _, tc := range tests { t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {…}) }`, plus `FuzzParse` with seed corpus for the same parser.

**Definition:** Type parameters on functions and types: `func Map[T, U any](xs []T, f func(T) U) []U`.
**Trade-offs:** Solves real boilerplate around containers and helpers, but Go's generics are deliberately limited (no specialisation, no operator overloading).
**Application Example:** A generic `slices.Filter[T any](s []T, keep func(T) bool) []T` replacing four near-identical filter functions for different concrete types.
Common gotchas (slice aliasing, defer eval, nil interface vs nil pointer)

**Definition:** Slice headers share underlying arrays (`s[1:3]` aliases `s`); `defer` evaluates **arguments** at the defer line, not at execution; a non-nil interface holding a nil pointer is **not nil** in `==`.
**Trade-offs:** Idiomatic Go relies on these — appending to a sub-slice can clobber the parent — but they trip every newcomer at least once.
**Application Example:** `var err *MyError = nil; var e error = err; e == nil // false` — the classic interview question that catches almost everyone the first time.

**Definition:** Production-quality building blocks: `net/http` (server + client), `sync.{Mutex, RWMutex, WaitGroup, Pool}`, `encoding/json` for marshaling.
**Trade-offs:** Wide enough for most workloads — Go services often ship with **only stdlib** — but `encoding/json` reflection is slow and `net/http` has subtle defaults (timeouts!).
**Application Example:** An HTTP service whose entire dependency footprint is the standard library: `net/http` for the server, `database/sql` for Postgres, `log/slog` for structured logs.
Python
children.note: Senior-level Python interview ground — the data model, the GIL, async, typing, packaging, and the gotchas that catch even experienced developers.

children.title:
**Definition:** Python's data model treats **everything as an object** with attributes and methods (ints, classes, modules, functions). Behaviour is implemented via **dunder methods** (`__init__`, `__add__`, `__iter__`) the language calls in response to operators and built-ins.
**Trade-offs:** Uniform semantics make the language deeply customisable — any class can be made hashable, comparable, iterable, callable — but the indirection costs CPU and the dunder vocabulary is large.
**Application Example:** Implementing `__len__` and `__iter__` on a custom collection so `len(c)`, `for x in c`, and `bool(c)` all work without writing extra code.

**Definition:** **Immutable** types (`int`, `float`, `str`, `tuple`, `frozenset`, `bytes`) cannot change after creation; **mutable** types (`list`, `dict`, `set`, most user classes) can. Immutable values are hashable and safe to share across threads/scopes.
**Trade-offs:** Immutability buys safety and hashability (usable as dict keys / set members) at the cost of allocating a new value on every "change"; mutability is efficient but creates aliasing bugs and disqualifies values from being keys.
**Application Example:** Using `tuple` of `(user_id, role)` as a dict key for caching permissions; using a `list` for the cache-misses queue because order matters and mutation is frequent.

**Definition:** Concise expressions building a new list/dict/set/generator from an iterable: `[x*x for x in xs if x > 0]`, `{k: v for k, v in pairs}`. **Generator expressions** `(x*x for x in xs)` are lazy — no list is built.
**Trade-offs:** More readable than `for`+`append` and faster (one CPython opcode), but nested comprehensions become unreadable past two levels — extract a helper function instead.
**Application Example:** `users_by_id = {u.id: u for u in users}` to build a lookup index in one line; `total = sum(x for x in stream if predicate(x))` to aggregate without materialising a list.

**Definition:** An **iterator** is anything with `__next__` / `__iter__`; a **generator** is a function using `yield` that returns an iterator and pauses/resumes between yields. `yield from` delegates to another iterable.
**Trade-offs:** Constant memory regardless of stream size and natural composition (chain generators), but only single-pass — once consumed, you must re-create. Errors mid-stream are harder to debug because the stack frame is the generator's, not the consumer's.
**Application Example:** `def read_logs(p): \n with open(p) as f: yield from f` streaming a 50 GB log file line-by-line; chained with `(line for line in read_logs(p) if 'ERROR' in line)` for filtering.

**Definition:** A callable that takes a function/class and returns a (usually wrapped) function/class. Applied with `@decorator` syntax sugar above the `def`/`class`.
**Trade-offs:** Concise way to add cross-cutting behaviour (logging, caching, auth) without modifying the wrapped code, but obscures what the function actually is — `functools.wraps` is mandatory to preserve `__name__`, `__doc__`, and signatures.
**Application Example:** `@functools.lru_cache(maxsize=1024)` to memoise an expensive pure function; `@app.route("/users")` (Flask) registering a handler; `@dataclass` synthesising `__init__`, `__repr__`, `__eq__`.

**Definition:** Objects with `__enter__` and `__exit__` methods (or wrapped via `@contextmanager`) that bracket a block — used with the `with` statement to guarantee setup/teardown even on exceptions.
**Trade-offs:** Cleaner than try/finally and composable (`with a, b:`), but stacking many context managers across a function quickly gets unreadable — `contextlib.ExitStack` helps for dynamic counts.
**Application Example:** `with open(path) as f, db.transaction(), tracer.span("import"):` running an import inside a transaction with a trace span — all three teardowns guaranteed even if the import raises.

**Definition:** Python resolves names by **LEGB** (Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in). A **closure** is an inner function capturing names from an enclosing function's scope. `nonlocal` rebinds an enclosing variable; `global` rebinds a module-level one.
**Trade-offs:** Closures make decorators and small callbacks idiomatic, but capture by **reference** not by value — the classic gotcha is `[lambda: i for i in range(3)]` producing three lambdas that all return `2`.
**Application Example:** `def make_adder(n): return lambda x: x + n` — `make_adder(5)(3) == 8`. The fix for the loop gotcha: `lambda i=i: ...` to capture the value at definition time via a default argument.
Common gotchas (mutable defaults, late-binding closures, copy vs deepcopy)

**Definition:** Three classics: (1) **mutable default arguments** (`def f(xs=[])` reuses the same list across calls), (2) **late-binding closures** (loops capturing the loop variable, not its value), (3) **copy vs deepcopy** (`list(d)` only copies the outer container).
**Trade-offs:** These behaviours are consistent with Python's evaluation rules but trip everyone at least once — linters (ruff, pylint) catch the first two automatically and `copy.deepcopy` is a one-import fix for the third.
**Application Example:** `def append(item, items=None): items = items or []; items.append(item); return items` instead of `items=[]`; `import copy; new = copy.deepcopy(old)` when the structure is nested mutable.

**Definition:** Multiple inheritance is supported; method resolution follows **C3 linearization** (`Class.__mro__`). `super()` walks the MRO, not just the immediate parent — call order is determined by the declaration order across all bases.
**Trade-offs:** Powerful for mixins (e.g. Django views), but the diamond problem and surprising `super()` ordering make deep multiple-inheritance brittle — composition via dataclasses + Protocols is often cleaner.
**Application Example:** Django generic views: `class MyView(LoginRequiredMixin, ListView)` — MRO ensures `dispatch()` calls `LoginRequiredMixin.dispatch()` first, then `ListView`'s, then the base `View`'s.

**Definition:** Double-underscore methods Python calls in response to syntax: `__init__` for construction, `__repr__` for debug output, `__eq__` and `__hash__` for equality/hashing, `__iter__`/`__next__` for iteration, `__enter__`/`__exit__` for context, `__call__` to make an instance callable.
**Trade-offs:** Lets user types feel native (any class can be hashable, callable, indexable), but easy to break invariants — defining `__eq__` without `__hash__` makes the class unhashable; defining `__hash__` without matching `__eq__` is a silent bug.
**Application Example:** A `Money` value class with `__eq__`, `__hash__`, `__add__`, `__repr__`, `__lt__` so `Money(10, "USD") + Money(5, "USD")` works and instances can live in sets and dict keys.
Type hints (typing, generics, Protocols, mypy / pyright)

**Definition:** Optional static type annotations (PEP 484+) via the `typing` module. **Not enforced at runtime** — `mypy` / `pyright` check them. Generics (`list[int]`, `Callable[[int], str]`), Protocols (structural typing), `TypedDict`, `Self`, `Annotated`.
**Trade-offs:** Massive boost to refactoring safety and editor help, especially in large codebases; the ecosystem still has gaps (missing stubs in old libraries) and type-correctness is a discipline, not a guarantee.
**Application Example:** A FastAPI endpoint signature `def create_user(payload: CreateUser, db: Session = Depends(get_db)) -> UserResponse:` — types drive request parsing, validation, response serialisation, AND IDE autocomplete.

**Definition:** Three libraries that synthesise `__init__`, `__repr__`, `__eq__`, etc. from class attributes. `dataclasses` (stdlib, minimal), `attrs` (older, more features, validators), **Pydantic** (heavier, runtime validation + JSON serialization, FastAPI's default).
**Trade-offs:** All three save boilerplate but pick by need — `dataclass` for simple value objects with no validation, `attrs` for advanced behaviour without runtime cost, **Pydantic** when data crosses an I/O boundary (HTTP, JSON, env).
**Application Example:** `@dataclass(frozen=True)` `class Point: x: float; y: float` for an internal value; `class CreateUser(BaseModel): email: EmailStr; age: int = Field(ge=0)` for a request body that needs validation at parse time.

**Definition:** The **Global Interpreter Lock** — CPython's mutex that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. Released around blocking I/O and many C-extension calls.
**Trade-offs:** Makes single-threaded Python fast and CPython internals simple, but caps **CPU-bound** Python code to one core regardless of thread count. PEP 703 (free-threaded CPython) is removing the GIL gradually starting in 3.13.
**Application Example:** A web server using threads is fine because each request waits on I/O (DB, network); a CPU-bound numerical pipeline benefits from `multiprocessing`, NumPy (drops the GIL inside C), or PEP 703 free-threaded mode.

**Definition:** Coroutines (`async def` functions) cooperatively yield control via `await`; an **event loop** schedules them. `asyncio.gather(*aws)` runs many concurrently in one thread.
**Trade-offs:** Massive throughput for I/O-heavy workloads (10k+ concurrent connections) on one core, but **colour-aware** — once a function is `async`, callers must be too; mixing sync libraries blocks the loop. CPU-bound work poisons the loop.
**Application Example:** An HTTPX scraper fetching 1000 URLs in parallel: `await asyncio.gather(*[client.get(u) for u in urls])` — sequential would be ~10 minutes; concurrent is ~10 seconds limited by network.

**Definition:** **Threading** runs concurrent tasks in one process under the GIL — good for **I/O-bound**. **Multiprocessing** spawns separate Python processes, each with its own GIL — good for **CPU-bound**; communicates via pickle.
**Trade-offs:** Threads are cheap but GIL-limited; processes give true parallelism at the cost of pickle overhead, slower startup, and shared state being painful (need `Manager`, `Queue`, or `multiprocessing.shared_memory`).
**Application Example:** `ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=32)` for hammering a remote API; `ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=os.cpu_count())` for parallel CPU-bound data transforms over a list of files.

**Definition:** High-level API over thread/process pools — `Executor.submit(fn)` returns a `Future`; `as_completed(futures)` iterates as results land; `executor.map(fn, iter)` is a parallel `map`.
**Trade-offs:** Drastically simpler than raw `threading`/`multiprocessing` for fan-out/fan-in, but offers limited control vs `asyncio` for I/O-bound work and is unsuitable for streaming/coroutine-style code.
**Application Example:** Parallel-downloading 100 files: `with ThreadPoolExecutor(20) as ex: list(ex.map(download, urls))` — 20 concurrent downloads, results in input order, exceptions raised on the consuming side.

**Definition:** CPython uses **reference counting** as the primary GC — objects deallocated as soon as their refcount hits zero. A separate **cyclic GC** runs periodically to break reference cycles (linked-list nodes, parent/child trees).
**Trade-offs:** Refcount gives deterministic cleanup (no surprise GC pauses for normal code) but adds per-op CPU cost on every assignment; cyclic GC handles cycles but its `gc.collect()` pause grows with the live-object count.
**Application Example:** Profiling memory with `tracemalloc` to find a leak; calling `gc.disable()` in a hot loop where you know there are no cycles, to skip generation-tracing overhead.
CPython performance (bytecode, C extensions, why Python is 'slow')

**Definition:** CPython compiles to **bytecode** (`.pyc`) executed by a stack-based interpreter. Speed comes from C extensions (NumPy, Pandas), the C API, and (since 3.11/3.12/3.13) faster interpreter and specialising adaptive optimisations.
**Trade-offs:** Productivity > raw speed for most apps, but pure-Python CPU-bound code is 10–100× slower than equivalent C/Go. Escape hatches: NumPy (vectorised C), Cython, mypyc, PyPy (alternative JIT), or rewriting hot paths in C/Rust via `cffi` / `PyO3`.
**Application Example:** A risk-calculation pipeline rewritten as `numpy` array ops drops from 8 minutes (pure-Python loops) to 4 seconds; a hot inner function moved to a Cython module gives a 30× speedup.

**Definition:** Tooling for installing dependencies and shipping projects: `pip` (installer), virtual environments (`venv`, `virtualenv`), `pyproject.toml` (PEP 621 metadata), and managers like **Poetry** / **uv** / **pdm** (resolution + locking).
**Trade-offs:** The ecosystem finally consolidated around `pyproject.toml`, but multiple competing tools (pip+venv vs Poetry vs uv) confuse newcomers. **uv** is currently the fastest and is rapidly winning adoption.
**Application Example:** `uv venv && uv pip install -r requirements.txt` for an existing pip project; `uv init && uv add fastapi uvicorn` for a new project — sub-second installs vs Poetry's tens of seconds.

**Definition:** **pytest** is the de-facto standard — function-style tests, fixtures (`@pytest.fixture`), parametrization (`@pytest.mark.parametrize`), huge plugin ecosystem. **unittest** is stdlib but verbose. **hypothesis** for property-based testing.
**Trade-offs:** pytest's fixtures are powerful but easy to over-engineer (deep fixture trees become hard to debug); parametrization scales test counts without scaling code; mocking via `unittest.mock` is pervasive but mock-heavy tests lock you to the implementation.
**Application Example:** `@pytest.mark.parametrize("input,expected", [(1, 1), (2, 4), (3, 9)])` for table-driven tests; `mocker.patch("app.repo.get_user", return_value=...)` (pytest-mock) replacing a DB call in a unit test.
CI/CD
children.note: Automating the path from commit to production with safety nets at every step.

children.title:
**Definition:** Continuous Integration: every commit triggers an automated pipeline — checkout → install deps → build → test → package → publish artifact.
**Trade-offs:** Catches integration breaks immediately and produces deployable artifacts on every green commit, but requires fast pipelines and a culture of fixing red builds first.
**Application Example:** A GitHub Actions workflow on every push: checkout, restore dep cache, run `go test ./…`, build a multi-arch container, push to ECR tagged with the commit SHA.
Branching strategies
children.note: How code flows from a developer's machine to main.

children.title:
**Definition:** All developers commit to a single mainline (`main`) at least daily; long-lived branches are forbidden. Risky changes hide behind feature flags.
**Trade-offs:** Eliminates merge hell and keeps the system continuously releasable, but demands a strong test suite and feature-flag discipline.
**Application Example:** Google and Meta scale: thousands of engineers committing to one trunk daily; feature flags gate unfinished work in production.

**Definition:** Long-lived `develop` and `main`, plus `feature/*`, `release/*`, and `hotfix/*` branches with formal merge rules.
**Trade-offs:** Maps cleanly to versioned releases and parallel maintenance, but heavy ceremony and merge complexity for fast-moving web apps.
**Application Example:** An on-prem product shipping a tagged release every quarter, maintaining `release/2024.01` for hotfixes while `develop` keeps moving.

**Definition:** Lightweight: branch from `main`, open a PR, get reviews/CI, merge to `main`, deploy. No `develop` branch.
**Trade-offs:** Simple and fits CD for SaaS, but assumes you can ship from `main` at any time — needs strong CI and feature flags.
**Application Example:** A typical SaaS team: every PR runs CI + preview env, gets one approval, squash-merges to main, and triggers an automated production deploy.

**Definition:** Cut a `release/x.y` branch when stabilising a version; only fixes flow into it, while `main` keeps moving forward.
**Trade-offs:** Lets you ship a stable version while continuing to develop, but you pay the merge tax of cherry-picking fixes between branches.
**Application Example:** A SDK team cutting `release/v3.2` to harden for a customer rollout while `main` already has v3.3 work; critical fixes cherry-picked to both.
Deployment strategies
children.note: How a new version takes traffic — risk vs. operational complexity.

children.title:
**Definition:** Stop the old version entirely, then start the new — guaranteed downtime during the swap.
**Trade-offs:** Simplest possible strategy with no version-skew concerns, but the downtime makes it unacceptable for most production services.
**Application Example:** A nightly batch processor with no live users, redeployed by stopping the old container and starting the new one once it's healthy.

**Definition:** Replace instances of the old version with the new one a few at a time, keeping enough capacity online to serve traffic.
**Trade-offs:** Zero-downtime by default and built into K8s/ECS, but you run mixed versions during the rollout — schemas and APIs must be backward-compatible.
**Application Example:** A K8s `Deployment` with `maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0` rolling 30 pods one at a time over 5 minutes, with `readinessProbe` gating each new pod.

**Definition:** Two parallel identical environments; new version goes to the idle one ("green"), traffic is switched at the LB once green is verified.
**Trade-offs:** Instant rollback (flip traffic back to blue), at the cost of doubling the running fleet during the cutover and stateful systems being hard to duplicate.
**Application Example:** An ALB with two target groups; deploys push to the inactive group, smoke tests run against it, then a target-group swap moves 100% of traffic in seconds.

**Definition:** Send a small percentage of traffic to the new version and monitor SLIs; ramp up gradually if it's healthy, roll back if not.
**Trade-offs:** Catches bugs that only show under real traffic with limited blast radius, but requires good observability + automated rollback to be safe.
**Application Example:** Argo Rollouts shifting 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% of `/checkout` traffic to v2 over 30 minutes, auto-aborting if `error_rate > 1%` or `p99 > 500ms`.

**Definition:** Production traffic is copied to the new version in parallel; responses from the shadow are **discarded**, so users see only the old version's response.
**Trade-offs:** Risk-free way to test the new version under real load and data, but doubles backend cost and breaks for non-idempotent traffic (writes).
**Application Example:** Mirroring 100% of the read traffic on `/search` to a new ranking service via Envoy `request_mirror_policies`, comparing latency/results offline before any real cutover.

**Definition:** Decouple deploy from release: ship code dark, then turn it on for users via a remote-controlled flag (per-user, per-tenant, per-region).
**Trade-offs:** Enables trunk-based development, instant kill switches, and incremental rollouts, but flags become technical debt if not retired aggressively.
**Application Example:** LaunchDarkly toggle gating a new pricing engine: deployed to all pods two weeks ago, enabled for 1% of traffic today, full rollout next week — kill switch one click away.

**Definition:** Distinct deploy targets — dev, staging, production — plus increasingly **ephemeral PR previews** for review and integration testing.
**Trade-offs:** Catches issues at progressively higher fidelity, but environment drift ("staging is different from prod") is a constant operational tax.
**Application Example:** A platform that spins up a per-PR environment via Terraform + ArgoCD on PR open, deletes it on close — reviewers click a unique URL to test the change.

**Definition:** Provision and manage cloud resources by code (Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation) rather than clicks; state is version-controlled and reviewable.
**Trade-offs:** Reproducible, auditable, and reviewable infrastructure, but state-file ownership and drift between code and reality are real operational concerns.
**Application Example:** A monorepo with `terraform/` modules for VPC, EKS, RDS, etc.; PRs run `terraform plan` in CI and merging triggers `apply` against a remote state in S3 + DynamoDB.

**Definition:** Git is the single source of truth for desired cluster state; an in-cluster operator (ArgoCD, Flux) reconciles the live state to match.
**Trade-offs:** Auditable change history and self-healing drift correction, but coupling deploy to git means broken git or operator outages block all deploys.
**Application Example:** ArgoCD watching `infra-repo/clusters/prod/` and continuously reconciling — a developer's PR merging into the manifests triggers a deploy 30s later, fully tracked in git.

**Definition:** Schema changes versioned with the code; **forward-only** migrations preferred, with the **expand-contract** pattern for breaking changes.
**Trade-offs:** Keeps schema in step with code and reviewable as PRs, but breaking changes require multi-deploy choreography (add column → backfill → switch reads → drop column).
**Application Example:** Adding a NOT NULL column without downtime: deploy 1 adds nullable col + backfill; deploy 2 makes app write it; deploy 3 makes it NOT NULL once 100% of rows are populated.

**Definition:** How you undo a bad deploy: redeploy the previous artifact, switch traffic back (blue/green), abort canary, or `kubectl rollout undo`.
**Trade-offs:** Fast rollback is the safety net that lets you ship aggressively, but stateful changes (DB migrations) often can't be cleanly rolled back.
**Application Example:** A canary auto-aborting and `kubectl rollout undo deployment/api` shifting traffic back to the previous ReplicaSet within 60 seconds of an error-rate spike.

**Definition:** Four metrics from the DORA / *Accelerate* research: **Deployment frequency**, **Lead time for changes**, **Change failure rate**, **Mean time to restore (MTTR)**.
**Trade-offs:** Empirically separates elite teams from low performers and is hard to game, but easy to misuse as a stick — context matters.
**Application Example:** A platform team's quarterly review showing deployment frequency rising from weekly → daily, lead time falling from 4 days → 4 hours, CFR holding at 8% — the Accelerate "high-performer" band.
